title: I Never Thought of It That Way

passage.title: I Never Thought of It That Way: How to Have Fearlessly Curious Conversations in Dangerously Divided Times (excerpt)
passage.author: By Mónica Guzmán
passage.text:
1. People tend to think of "perspectives" as different views on the same thing. That's always seemed a bit [word] to me. People do the viewing, right? Unique people walking unique paths that led them to those views? We don't see with our eyes, after all, but with our whole biographies. It's time our sense of perspective took that into account.

2. The Scots have a word related to the [word] of our knowledge that I find super wise. It's a "fossil word," which means (fun fact!) that it's essentially dead to language except for its use in one surviving phrase. The word is "ken," and the phrase is "beyond one's ken.” The whole concept was born on the high seas.

3. When Scottish sailors first talked about their "ken" in the 1500s, they were talking about how far they could see to the horizon out on the water. Then writers began to use "ken" to refer first to the range of your vision—at sea or not—and then, to the range of your knowledge or understanding. To what your mind can see, and not just your eyes. "One's range of knowledge or sight," goes the dictionary definition of ken. If something is "beyond my ken," it's beyond my understanding. I don't see it, so I can't know it. It's beyond my horizon. It's beyond me.

4. What I love about this word is that it draws our knowledge with obvious bounds and limits. It's [word]. [word]. Something we can reach the end of. "Ken" reminds us of something important: you can't know what you aren't close to.

5. Look beyond your ken, and you won't see anything clearly. Sometimes you won't see certain things at all. [...] Other times, you'll see the barest hints of shapes out there beyond your ken. Shapes that'll seem neutral, interesting, or threatening. When they seem neutral, you'll shrug 'em off. When they seem interesting, you'll look a couple times, and maybe move closer to get a better look. When those distant shapes seem threatening, though, you won't want to move closer or move on. You'll want to run away, [word], or even attack.

6. It's weird to do anything drastic when you can barely make out the thing that's scaring you. So you'll do something to resolve that: You'll __manufacture__ certainty. You'll convince yourself that the shape you see beyond your ken fits the description of that sea monster everyone in your [word]'s been buzzing about. And you'll fight, flee, or rage accordingly.

7. When you fill in those gaps in your perception, you're motivated by what scientists call NFC—your need for closure. And you want to reach what's called "[word] closure," the “desire for a firm answer to a question and an [word] toward [word]." You can't see everything, so you'll tell yourself you've seen enough.

8. What to do about this? It would make sense to shrug and say to yourself, *Well, it's not like I can step outside my own perspective*. But you can.

9. Asking "What am I missing?" is the first step. It's that doorstop against cognitive closure that comes too early, that hasn't gotten close enough to know enough at all.

questions.question_content: **Which evidence from the text best explains *why* the author thinks we need to adjust our understanding of what *perspective* means?**

choices.label: A
choices.content: “People do the viewing, right? Unique people walking unique paths that led them to those views? We don't see with our eyes, after all, but with our whole biographies.” (paragraph 1)

choices.label: B
choices.content: “The word is ‘ken,’ and the phrase is ‘beyond one's ken.’ The whole concept was born on the high seas.” (paragraph 2)

choices.label: C
choices.content: “Then writers began to use "ken" to refer first to the range of your vision—at sea or not—and then, to the range of your knowledge or understanding.” (paragraph 3)

choices.label: D
choices.content: “It's weird to do anything drastic when you can barely make out the thing that's scaring you.” (paragraph 6)
